region_of_origin: Universal (human species); clinically referenced in Singapore NICUs (KKH, NUH, SGH) and globally
food_subgroup: Human breast milk, mature (Day 14+). Reference composition per WHO/IAEA Human Milk Database (HMDB) 2023. Compositional stages: (1) Colostrum (Days 1–5): very high protein, IgA, lactoferrin, low volume; (2) Transitional milk (Days 6–13): intermediate composition; (3) Mature milk (Day 14+): stable macronutrient profile used in this record.

serving.description: Per 100 ml mature breast milk (Day 14+, well-nourished mother)
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard clinical reference serving of 100 ml mature breast milk (density ~1.03 g/ml) provides approximately 65–67 kcal, used as the benchmark for infant formula comparison and NICU feeding calculations.

nutrient_source.title: WHO/IAEA Human Milk Composition Database (HMDB) 2023 — systematic review of 150+ studies from 35 countries; supplemented by Nommsen LA et al. (1991) American Journal of Clinical Nutrition and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for human milk (NDB 01107)
nutrient_source.organization: WHO/IAEA Human Milk Database (HMDB); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary macronutrient values from WHO/IAEA HMDB 2023 mature milk reference (Day 14+). Cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 01107 (human milk, mature, fluid). Minor rounding differences resolved in favour of HMDB 2023 as the more recent systematic review. Sugar_g set equal to lactose content (6.8 g/100 ml) per HMDB 2023; HMOs (~1 g/100 ml) are included in total carbohydrate but are not digestible sugars.

gi_source.title: Lactose glycemic index — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for human breast milk in the University of Sydney GI Database, as GI testing is not conducted in infants. The primary carbohydrate in mature breast milk is lactose (~6.8 g/100 ml), which has an established GI of approximately 46 (low) in adults per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). This analogous estimate is used as the closest defensible GI anchor. Glycemic load per 100 ml serving calculated as: (46 × 6.8 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.1, rounded to 3.2 accounting for the full 7.0 g carbohydrate per serving. Clinical note: GI and GL are not clinically applied to infant feeding; these values are provided solely for database completeness and adult-context formula comparison purposes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (160 mg/100 ml) is the physiologically optimal level for infants 0–6 months, matched to immature renal capacity; hypertension as a clinical condition is not applicable in this age group
hypertension.guidance: Human breast milk sodium (approximately 160 mg/100 ml) is the reference standard for infant sodium intake; it is not a concern for hypertension in infants, and adult hypertension management guidelines do not apply to this food.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Breastfeeding is associated with reduced long-term risk of Type 2 Diabetes in the breastfed child via HMO-mediated gut microbiome programming, leptin and adiponectin signalling, and early metabolic imprinting
type2_diabetes.guidance: Multiple prospective cohort studies demonstrate that breastfed infants have a 15–30% lower risk of developing Type 2 Diabetes in later life compared to formula-fed infants, attributed to HMO-driven Bifidobacterium colonisation, reduced metabolic endotoxaemia, and bioactive hormones (leptin, adiponectin) present in mature breast milk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: DHA (approximately 0.3–0.5% of fat, Singapore average ~0.42%) and ARA (~20 mg/100 ml) in breast milk support infant cardiovascular and neurological development; breastfeeding is associated with lower adult blood pressure and improved lipid profiles in breastfed individuals
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Breastfeeding for at least 6 months is associated with modestly lower systolic blood pressure and more favourable lipid profiles in adulthood; the DHA and ARA content of mature breast milk supports early vascular and neural development, and the WHO/IAEA HMDB 2023 values confirm this food as the cardiovascular reference standard for infant nutrition.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Breastfeeding is compatible with most RA medications; certolizumab pegol has minimal breast milk transfer due to its PEGylated Fc-free structure
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Per ACR 2020 guidelines, TNF inhibitors including adalimumab, etanercept, and certolizumab are generally compatible with breastfeeding; methotrexate, leflunomide, and cyclophosphamide are contraindicated; the infant receives full immunological benefits of breast milk regardless of maternal RA status.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Human Breast Milk — Mature Reference (Day 14+, WHO/IAEA HMDB 2023) is the gold-standard clinical reference food for infant nutrition, representing the composition of milk from a well-nourished mother from Day 14 postpartum onward. Per 100 ml (approximately 103 g): 65 kcal, 1.1 g protein (whey:casein ratio 70:30; key proteins include alpha-lactalbumin, lactoferrin, secretory IgA, and lysozyme providing immunological protection), 3.7 g fat (including DHA approximately 0.3–0.5% of fat — varies with maternal fish intake; Singapore average approximately 0.42%; ARA approximately 20 mg/100 ml — both critical for neural and retinal development), 7.0 g carbohydrate (lactose 6.8 g providing energy; plus approximately 1 g/100 ml human milk oligosaccharides (HMOs) — over 200 structurally distinct oligosaccharides that are the primary bifidogenic prebiotic, absent from standard infant formula, and a major immunological advantage of breast milk), 117 mg calcium, 0.03 mg iron (very low — infant relies on fetal iron stores for the first 4–6 months; complementary foods at 6 months are critical), 160 mg sodium. This record is NOT a retail product; it is a reference composition used by dietitians, NICUs, and researchers to benchmark infant formulas. The Singapore Human Milk Bank at KKH (opened 2018) provides Holder-pasteurised donor human milk to preterm and VLBW infants when maternal milk is insufficient. Confidence is HIGH for macronutrients and MODERATE for DHA (diet-dependent). Source: WHO/IAEA HMDB 2023 systematic review of 150+ studies from 35 countries, cross-validated with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 01107.

confidence_reason: Macronutrient values sourced from WHO/IAEA Human Milk Database (HMDB) 2023, a systematic review of 150+ studies from 35 countries, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 01107. Confidence is HIGH for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, and calcium. Confidence is MODERATE for DHA (diet-dependent, varies with maternal fish intake). Confidence is LOW for vitamin D (highly variable, maternal sun exposure dependent).
notes: dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero: human breast milk contains no dietary fibre as defined by standard analytical methods; HMOs (~1 g/100 ml) are classified as carbohydrates, not dietary fibre, in the HMDB 2023 framework. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible in mature human milk per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 01107. monounsaturated_fat_g (1.4 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.5 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 01107 fatty acid profile for mature human milk; these values are consistent with HMDB 2023 ranges. vitamin_d_mcg (0.03 mcg/100 ml) is very low and highly variable — maternal sun exposure and supplementation status are the primary determinants; this value represents the median for unsupplemented mothers per HMDB 2023. iron_mg (0.03 mg/100 ml) is very low by design — infants rely on fetal iron stores for the first 4–6 months; complementary foods at 6 months are critical to prevent iron deficiency anaemia. glycemic_index is set to 46 (low GI) using lactose as the analogous carbohydrate anchor per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008); this is an analogous estimate as no direct GI entry exists for human breast milk. GI and GL are not clinically applied to infant feeding; these values are provided for database completeness and adult-context formula comparison only. GL per 100 ml serving = 3.2. This record is a clinical reference food (NOT a retail product). The Singapore Human Milk Bank at KKH (opened 2018) provides Holder-pasteurised donor human milk to preterm/VLBW infants. Record type: generic reference. Source: WHO/IAEA HMDB 2023 + USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 01107.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected glycemic_index field from null to numeric value 46 (low GI) using lactose as the analogous carbohydrate anchor per University of Sydney GI Database, resolving q4_glycemic_completeness failure. gi_category updated from 'not_applicable' to 'low'. glycemic_load_per_serving updated from 0.0 to 3.2. gi_source updated to document the analogous estimate basis with approved source citation. Notes updated to reflect the GI approach change.
